exercises.title: Why the KV cache exists
exercises.prompt: A naive autoregressive decoder is O(N²) to generate N tokens because it recomputes attention over the whole prefix each step. Explain what the KV cache stores, why it makes per-step decoding O(N) instead, and why you cache K and V but not Q.
exercises.hint: Past tokens' keys/values are fixed once computed. Each new step only needs the new token's query against all cached K/V. Old queries are never reused.
exercises.solution:
**The waste without a cache:** to generate token `t`, naive decoding re-runs the full forward pass over the entire prefix `0..t-1`, recomputing every past token's keys and values from scratch — even though those are **deterministic and unchanged**. Over N tokens that's O(N²) total work, most of it redundant.

**What the KV cache stores:** the **keys (K) and values (V) of every past token, at every layer and head**, kept in memory across decoding steps. Once token `i`'s K and V are computed, they never change, so you compute them **once** and reuse them.

**Why it makes each step O(N):** at step `t` you only need to:
1. compute the **new token's Q, K, V** (one token's worth of work),
2. **append** its K,V to the cache,
3. run the new token's **query against all cached keys/values** (an O(t) dot-product), softmax, weighted sum.
No recomputation of the prefix. Per-step cost is O(prefix length) instead of O(prefix²); generating N tokens is O(N²) *reads* but with a tiny constant — the expensive recompute is gone, and in practice decode becomes **memory-bandwidth-bound on reading the cache**, not compute-bound.

**Why K and V but not Q:** a **query** represents 'what *this* token is looking for' and is only used **at the step that token is generated** — once you've produced token `t`, its query is never needed again. **Keys and values**, by contrast, represent 'what each past token offers' and are read by **every future token's** query. So K and V are reused (cache them); Q is consumed immediately (don't). The cache size = `2 (K,V) × layers × n_kv_heads × d_head × seq_len × batch` — which is why **GQA/MQA** (fewer KV heads, Lesson 03) matters so much for long context.

exercises.title: Flash Attention: same math, different memory traffic
exercises.prompt: Flash Attention computes exactly the same attention output as standard attention but is far faster. Explain the key insight (it's memory-bound, not FLOP-bound), what standard attention materializes that Flash avoids, and how tiling + the online softmax let it never write the N×N matrix to HBM.
exercises.hint: Standard attention writes the N×N scores + softmax to slow HBM. Flash tiles Q/K/V into blocks kept in fast SRAM, computes softmax incrementally (running max + running sum), never materializing the full matrix. IO-aware, not fewer FLOPs.
exercises.solution:
**The key insight:** for N ≥ ~2K, attention is **memory-bandwidth-bound, not FLOP-bound**. The arithmetic is cheap relative to the cost of **moving data** between the GPU's big-but-slow **HBM** and its small-but-fast **SRAM**. Standard kernels underuse the GPU by 4–10× because they shuttle huge intermediate tensors to and from HBM.

**What standard attention materializes (and Flash avoids):** the textbook implementation writes the full **N×N score matrix** `S = QKᵀ/√d` to HBM, reads it back to apply softmax, writes the **N×N probability matrix** `P`, reads it back to compute `P·V`. Those N×N reads/writes to HBM are the bottleneck — and the N×N matrix is also the O(N²) *activation memory* that blows up long-context training.

**How Flash Attention avoids it — tiling + online softmax:**
- **Tiling:** split Q, K, V into **blocks** small enough to fit in **SRAM**. Loop over key/value blocks for each query block, loading only a tile at a time.
- **Online (streaming) softmax:** you can't normalize a softmax until you've seen all scores — *unless* you track running statistics. Flash keeps, per query row, a **running max `m`** and a **running denominator `ℓ`** (sum of exp), plus the **running weighted output**. As each new K/V tile arrives, it **rescales** the accumulated output by the corrected max and adds the new tile's contribution — mathematically identical to computing the full softmax, but **incrementally**.
- **Result:** the full N×N matrix is **never written to HBM** — only small tiles live in SRAM, and only the final `(N×d)` output goes to HBM. Memory traffic drops from O(N²) to roughly O(N), activation memory for attention becomes **O(N)** (linear), and the backward pass recomputes tiles instead of storing the matrix.

**Crucial point:** Flash Attention does **the same FLOPs and produces the exact same output** (not an approximation) — it's an **IO-aware** kernel that wins purely by reducing HBM traffic. That linear activation memory is what made long-context training/inference (32K, 128K+) practical, and it composes with the KV cache at decode time.

exercises.title: Training vs inference bottlenecks, and the memory budget
exercises.prompt: The lesson's framing: 'Training is parallel and FLOP-bound; inference is serial and memory-bound.' Unpack this. Explain prefill vs decode phases, why decode is memory-bandwidth-bound, how KV cache size limits batch/context, and which techniques (Flash, KV cache, GQA/MQA, paged attention, quantized KV) attack which bottleneck.
exercises.hint: Prefill = parallel over the prompt (compute-bound, Flash helps). Decode = one token at a time, re-reading weights+KV cache from HBM per step (bandwidth-bound). KV cache grows with batch×context, capping how much you can serve. Map each trick to its bottleneck.
exercises.solution:
**Training is FLOP-bound and parallel.** You process whole sequences (and big batches) at once; the GPU is saturated with large matmuls over the full sequence, and the limiter is **arithmetic throughput**. Long-context training's pain point is **activation memory** (the O(N²) attention matrix) — which **Flash Attention** removes by making attention memory linear.

**Inference has two phases:**
1. **Prefill** — process the entire **input prompt** in one parallel forward pass (and fill the KV cache). This is **compute-bound**, much like training; **Flash Attention** speeds it up.
2. **Decode** — generate output tokens **one at a time**, each depending on the last. This is **serial** and **memory-bandwidth-bound**: each step does very little compute (one new token) but must **read the entire model's weights and the whole KV cache from HBM** to produce a single token. You're limited by **how fast you can stream bytes**, not by FLOPs — the GPU's compute units sit mostly idle.

**Why the KV cache is the central constraint at decode:** its size is `2 × layers × n_kv_heads × d_head × seq_len × batch`. It grows **linearly with both context length and batch size**, and at long context it can exceed the model weights themselves. This caps **how many concurrent requests (batch) and how long a context** you can serve in a given amount of HBM, and every decode step must re-read it — so it drives both **memory capacity** and **bandwidth** limits.

**Mapping techniques to bottlenecks:**
- **Flash Attention** → *prefill/training compute + activation memory*: same output, far less HBM traffic, linear attention memory. Helps decode less (decode's matrix is tiny) but is the workhorse for prefill and long-context training.
- **KV cache** → *decode compute*: eliminates O(N²) recomputation, the precondition for fast autoregressive generation.
- **GQA / MQA** (Lesson 03) → *decode bandwidth + KV memory*: fewer KV heads → a **smaller KV cache** → less to stream per step and room for longer context / bigger batch. Nearly free quality with GQA.
- **Paged attention (vLLM)** → *KV memory fragmentation*: stores the cache in fixed-size **pages** (like virtual memory) so you don't pre-reserve max-length contiguous buffers, enabling higher batch occupancy and efficient sharing (e.g. shared prompts).
- **Quantized KV cache** (e.g. FP8/INT8 keys/values) → *KV memory + bandwidth*: halve/quarter the cache bytes, trading a little accuracy for longer context / bigger batch and faster reads.
- **Speculative decoding** (Lesson 16) → *decode latency*: amortizes the per-step weight read across multiple tokens verified in one forward pass.
- **MoE** (Lesson 11) → *FLOPs*, but **adds** KV/memory pressure — orthogonal axis.

**The synthesis:** know your phase. Training/prefill: cut FLOPs and activation memory (Flash, MoE, efficient kernels). Decode: cut **bytes moved per token** — shrink the KV cache (GQA/MQA, quantization, paging), avoid recompute (KV cache), and produce more tokens per weight-read (speculative decoding). Optimizing the wrong bottleneck (e.g. chasing FLOPs to speed up bandwidth-bound decode) wins nothing.
